text: Lecture	  10	  –	  Connective	  tissue	  matrix	  and	  fibres	  	   Learning	  outcomes	  	   • To	  describe	  the	  “normal”	  structure	  and	  function	  of	  key	  structural	  proteins	  in	  the	  Extra	  Cellular	  Matrix.	   • Describe	  the	  important	  components	  and	  functions	  of	  the	  Extra	  Cellular	  Matrix	  (ECM).	   • Describe	  the	  structure	  and	  function	  of	  Collagen	  in	  connective	  tissue.	   • Explain	  the	  biosynthesis	  of	  Collagen	  as	  an	  example	  of	  a	  complex	  connective	  tissue	  protein.	   • Describe	  the	  structure	  and	  function	  of	  Elastin,	  Fibrillin,	  Fibronectin	  and	  Laminin.	   • Recognise	  what	  happens	  when	  these	  proteins	  do	  not	  function	  normally.	   	   Connective	  Tissue	  	   Definition	   • Tissue	  concerned	  with	  supporting	  structures	  and	  binding	  parts	  together	   • Also	  includes	  tissue	  involved	  in	  blood	  formation	  and	  defence	  mechanisms	   	   Groups	  of	  CT	   • Vascular	  tissue	  	  -­‐	  lining	  of	  vessel	  walls	  	   • CT	  proper	   • Cartilage	  –	  articular	  surfaces	   • Bone	  	   	   Roles	  	   Important	  role	  in	  normal	  and	  pathological	  processes	  	   • Tensile	  strength	  	  (ligaments,	  bone	  cartilage)	   • Pliable	  (curvable,	  elasticity)	   • Control	  of	  fluid	  movement	  -­‐	  molecular	  sieve	   • Tissue	  repair	  (wound	  healing)	   • Mineral	  deposition	  (esp.	  bone)	   • Cell	  differentiation	   • Cancer	  	  -­‐	  changes	  in	  some	  of	  cell	  surface	  markers	  allows	  cancerous	  cells	  to	  move	  through	  and	  divide	  in	   the	  ECM.	   • Other	  disease	  states	  –	  esp	  anything	  to	  do	  with	  inflammation	   	   Extracellular	  Matrix	   • Surround	  cells	   • Secreted	  by	  CT	  cells	  	   • Components	  determine	  the	  nature	  and	  function	  of	  CT	   • Mechanical	  and	  support	  functions	  –	  EC	  insoluble	  protein	  fibres,	  formed	  by	  high	  MW	   polymers/proteins	  embedded	  in	  matrix	   • 3	  major	  classes	  of	  biomolecules	  –	  glycoproteins,	  proteoglycans	  and	  glycolipids	   o Types	  of	  glycoproteins:	    key	  structural	  proteins	  –	  collagen,	  elastin	  and	  fibrillin	  	   	    specialized	  proteins:	  fibrin,	  fibronectin	  and	   laminin	   Collagen	  	   • Most	  abundant	  protein	  in	  animal	  world	   • 25-­‐30%	  body	  protein	  (~3kg	  in	  body)	  	   o compared	  to	  600g	  Hb	  and	  150g	  Albumin	   • Makes	  up	  the	  organic	  matter	  of	  bone	  	   • Has	  the	  tensile	  strength	  of	  steel	   	   	   	   	   Types	  	   • 15-­‐20	  distinct	  types,	  each	  made	  of	  a	  combination	  up	  to	  30	  different	  polypeptide	  chains/subunits	   • Almost	  all	  of	  which	  have	  a	  different	  gene	   • Different	  types	  have	  different	  properties	  and	  thus	  used	  for	  different	  function	   • Major	  Types	   o Type	  1	  –	  tendons,	  ligaments,	  bone	  and	  skin	  	   o Type	  2	  –	  50%	  of	  protein	  in	  cartilage,	  vitreous	  humor	  of	  eye	  	   o Type	  3	  –	  skin,	  artery	  walls,	  intestines	  and	  uterus	  	   o Type	  4	  –	  Basal	  Lamina	  (filter	  for	  capillaries	  and	  glomeruli)	  	   o Type	  9	  –	  cartilage	   These	  are	  the	  most	  abundant,	  but	  the	  others	  are	  equally	  important	  	   AA	  comp	   • glycine,	  alanine,	  proline,	  lysine	  	   • unique	  AA’s	  to	  collagen	  –	  hydroxyproline	  and	  hydroylysine	  (post-­‐ trans	  mod)	   • proportions	  of	  AA	  determine	  the	  properties	  	   	   Hydroxyproline	   • Mainly	  found	  in	  collagen	  	   • Post-­‐translational	  modification	  (add	  OH	  group)	   • Vit	  C	  as	  co-­‐factor	  for	  synthesis	   • Confers	  rigidity	  to	  collagen	  as	  it	  allows	  H-­‐bond	  formation	   	   Structure	  of	  Collagen	   Primary	  structure	  	   • The	  basic	  unit	  is	  a	  repeating	  polypeptide	  sequence	  (Gly	  –X	  –Y)	   o X	  =	  proline	  (usually)	   o Y	  =hydroxyproline	  (usually)	   o Pretty	  much	  every	  3rd	  AA	  is	  glycine	   	   Secondary	  structure	  	   • Elongated,	  Left-­‐handed	  helix	  (direction	  unique	  to	  collagen)	   • N	  and	  C	  terminals	  have	  globular	  domains	  =	  soluble	  	   • Processed	  in	  ER	  and	  golgi	   o Glycosylated	   o OH	  added	  (hydroxylation)	   o Disulphide	  (S-­‐S)	  bonds	  link	  3	  primary	  chains	  covalently	  which	  twist	  to	  form	  triple	  helix	   Tertiary	  structure	  	   The	  triple	  helix	  formed	  when	  three	  chains	  twist	  together	  as	  described	  above	   • Helix	  may	  be	  formed	  by	  different	  types	  of	  collagens.	   • Eg.	  eg	  type	  2	  –	  all	  the	  same,	  type	  1	  –	  2	  the	  same	  and	  one	  similar	   	   Quaternary	  structure	  	   • When	  triple	  helix	  (procollagen)	  is	  secreted	  from	  cell	  (fibroblast)	  the	  globular	  ends	  are	  cleaved	  off.	   o The	  N	  and	  C	  terminals	  are	  cleaved	   • Linear,	  insoluble	  molecules	  assembles	  into	  collagen	  strands	   o Do	  so	  in	  a	  staggered	  pattern	  that	  gives	  rise	  to	  striations	  (clearly	  visible)	   o H	  bonds	  and	  covalent	  cross	  links	  hold	  it	  in	  place	   • Type	  4	  is	  an	  exception	  it	  forms	  a	  meshwork	   	   Biosynthesis	   	   • A	  series	  of	  post-­‐translational	  modification.	  	   • Intracellular	   o Hydroxylation	   o Glycosylation	  of	  OH-­‐lysine	  residues	   o Formation	  of	  intra/inter	  chain	  S-­‐S	  bonds	   o Formation/packaging	  of	  triple	  sequence	   • Extracellular	   o Removal	  of	  Pro-­‐sequences	   o Hydrogen	  bond	  formation	   o Covalent	  cross-­‐links	   o Fibre	  formation	   Catabolism	  	   • Collagenase	  cleaves	  chain	  ~3/4	  length	  from	  N	  terminus	  	   • Degraded	  later	  by	  extracellular	  proteases	   o Proteases	  are	  important	  in	  disease	  state	  –	  eg	  inflammation,	  vasculitis,	  chronic	  ulceration	   • Adult	  collagen	  ~300d	  1/2life,	  length	  of	  ½	  life	  is	  determined	  by	  activity	  of	  proteases	   	   Inherited	  diseases	  caused	  by	  collagen	  genes	  mutation	  	   • Brittle	  bone	  disease	  	  -­‐	  Osteogenesis	  Imperfecta	  (type	  1)	   • Dwarfish	  (type	  2)	   • Rubber	  man	  syndrome:	  Ehler’s-­‐Danlos	  syndrome	  (type	  1/3)	   	   Other	  disease	  	   • Scurvy	  =	  Vit	  C	  deficiency	  	   • Vit	  C	  is	  essential	  co-­‐factor	  for	  hydroxylation	  of	  proline,	  so	  patient	  is	  unable	  to	  synth	  collagen	  (and	  other	   CT)	  properly	   • Bleeding	  gums	  and	  loose	  teeth	   Elastin	  	   • Principle	  protein	  of	  yellow	  CT	   • Confers	  extensibility	  and	  recoil	  on	  lung,	  BVs	  and	  ligaments	  	   • Single	  gene	  encoding	  for	  elastin	   Normal'Systems:'Musculoskeletal'System'Notes'Semester'2,'2010' ! Procollagen!is!secreted! from!the!cell! 	   AA	  composition	  	   • ~1/3	  glycine	  	   • Alanine,	  valine,	  proline	  and	  hydroxyproline	   	   Structure	  	   • Major	  crosslinks	  –	  desmosines	   o Formed	  by	  condensation	  of	  3	  Lysine	  derived	  aldehydes	   with	  an	  unmodified	  Lysine	  (4-­‐way	  crosslinking)	  	   o desmosine	  cross-­‐links	  and	  fibrillar	  ‘oiled’	  coils	  provide	   elasticity	  of	  the	  molecule	  	   	   Disease	  	   • emphysema	  	  =	  uncontrolled	  degradation	  of	  elastin,	  decrease	  in	   lung	  SA	   	   Fibrillin	  (Glycoprotein)	   • large	  glycoprotein	  (350kDa)	   • Structural	  comp	  of	  micro-­‐fibrils	  	   • Secreted	  into	  ECM	  by	  fibroblasts	  	   • Scaffold	  for	  elastin	  deposition	  (as	  incorporation	  into	  insoluble	  micro-­‐fibrils)	   	   Disease	  	   • Marfan	  syndrome	   o Affects	  all	  organs:	  skeleton,	  lungs,	  heart,	  BVs	  and	  eyes	   o Dissecting	  aortic	  aneurysm	  –	  fatal	  if	  rupture	   	   Fibronectin	  (Glycoprotein)	   • Major	  glycoprotein	  of	  ECM	  (also	  in	  plasma	  in	  soluble	  form)	  	   • 2	  identical	  subunits,	  joined	  S-­‐S,	  near	  their	  carboxyl	  terminals	  	   • Large,	  can	  be	  seen	  in	  electron-­‐microscope	   • Functional	  domains	  bind	  to	  heparin,	  fibrin,	  collagen,	  DNA	  and	  cell	   surfaces	   	   Function	  	   • Anchors	  cells	  to	  collagen	  fibre/ECM	   • Receptor	  binding	  	   • Affects	  cell	  behaviour	   • Involved	  in	  cell	  migration,	  by	  providing	  binding	  site	  for	  cells	  	   • Decreased	  fibronectin	  around	  transformed	  (tumour)	  cells	  –	  partly	  explains	   faulty	  interaction	  with	  ECM	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Laminin	  (Glycoprotein)	   • Associated	  with	  basement	  membrane	  (accounts	  for	  many	   functional	  properties	  of	  the	  BM)	   • 3	  distinct	  elongated	  polypeptide	  chains	   • Binding	  sites	  for	  type	  4	  collagen,	  heparin	  (proteoglycan),	  integrins	  	   • Collagen	  interacts	  with	  laminin,	  which	  interacts	  with	  integrins	  and	  other	  laminin	  receptor	  proteins	   	   Function	  	   • Main	  anchor	  point	  for	  cells	  in	  ECM	   • Diff	  of	  cells	   • Nerve	  regeneration	  	   • Cell	  migration	  and	  proliferation	  (these	  don’t	  occur	  at	  same	  time)	   • Tumour	  cells	  –	  proteolytic	  fragments	  (of	  laminin)	  inhibits	  metastasis,	  whilst	  whole	  molecule	  stimulates	  it.	   	   	   Degradation	  of	  ECM	  	   • Localised	  degradation	  of	  ECM	  allows	  cells	  to	  migrate	  through	  basal	  lamina	  	   o Import	  for	  tissue	  repair,	  migration	  of	  WBC	   o Specialized	  enzyme	  required,	  which	  tightly	  regulate	  degradation	  (serine	  proteases,	  collagenases,	   plasminogen	  activators,	  plasmin)	  	   • Uncontrolled	  degradation	  of	  ECM	  (enzyme	  deregulation)	  =	  disease	  	   o Usually	  seen	  in	  chronic	  inflammatory	  disorders	  of	  bone,	  cartilage	  and	  skin	  	    Osteomyelitis	    Rheumatoid	  Arthritis	    Chronic	  leg	  ulcers	    Periodontitis
